text: The surgeon attempts to take down thick adhesions at the inferior edge of the gallbladder, with difficulty in distinguishing the duodenum and transverse colon due to the density of the adhesions. The gallbladder wall begins to become visible.
The surgeon uses indocyanine green (ICG) fluorescence to visualize the cystic duct and gallbladder wall, enhancing the identification of dissection planes.
The surgeon removes the drain site to increase the mobility of the gallbladder over the liver edge, facilitating further dissection.
The video shows ICG fluorescence within the bile, indicating that the cystic duct has reopened.

The surgeon attempts to free adhesions at the bottom of the gallbladder, aiming to dissect within the hepatocystic triangle. The common duct and cystic duct are visible, facilitating the identification of these structures for further dissection.
The surgeon attempts to access the bottom of the gallbladder near the cystic duct, encountering a thick layer of adhesions in the peritoneum.
The surgeon elevates the gallbladder, inadvertently tenting the common bile duct, while dissecting at a lower position than intended.
The surgeon uses ICG fluorescence to visualize the cystic duct, but expresses discomfort with the current dissection approach.

The surgeon encounters minor bleeding during the dissection of the gallbladder and decides to abort this part of the procedure. A top-down approach is initiated, which is challenging due to scarring from previous interventions and difficulty accessing the liver bed.
The surgeon uses a sponge to minimize bleeding and maintain a dry surgical field while controlling the bleeding.
The surgeon continues to detach the gallbladder from the liver using a top-down approach, maneuvering through thick adhesions visible in the peritoneum.
The surgeon attempts to reposition into the proper dissection plane, moving away from the liver to minimize bleeding during the cholestectomy procedure.
Using ICG imaging, the surgeon identifies the gallbladder wall and liver bed, carefully dissecting to expose the cystic duct and artery as the remaining attachments of the gallbladder to the liver.
The surgeon encounters feeding vessels during the dissection near the gallbladder and uses bipolar cautery to control them, highlighting the presence of multiple vessels in the area.
The surgeon uses bipolar forceps and scissors to control bleeding while working down towards the junction of the cystic duct and the common duct, which is clearly visible.
The surgeon performs inferior dissection to improve gallbladder mobility, opening the enveloping peritoneum at the gallbladder's base to isolate the cystic duct, which is now more clearly visible.
The surgeon employs a top-down approach to the dissection, clearly visualizing the cystic duct. The focus shifts to identifying the cystic artery proper amidst minimal bleeding.
The surgeon carefully dissects behind the cystic duct, using precise movements to separate it from surrounding tissue. The cystic artery has not yet been visually identified.

The surgeon identifies the cystic artery positioned higher in the triangle than the cystic duct. With careful dissection, the artery is located without the need for ICG enhancement. The surgeon creates a window behind the cystic duct for further dissection.

The surgeon uses ICG to enhance visualization during dissection, clearing tissue from the back of the cystic duct to identify the cystic artery. A crossing vessel, identified as one of the hepatic arteries, is visible. The surgeon creates a window behind the cystic artery at its junction with the hepatic artery.
The surgeon clips the cystic artery and duct, placing one clip on the artery and two clips on the duct, including the specimen side of each. Hemostasis is achieved, concluding the procedure.
